segment-text: John McNamara Based on the Biography by Bruce Cook
TRUMBO PRODUCTIONS, LLC
As white words FADE UP in silence -Later, you might ask, "Wait, that really happened?" A beat, then -It really happened. A longer beat, then -And it mostly happened like this...
EXT. LAZY-T RANCH - 1947 - DAWN
Alone in a vast, serrated mountain range a hundred miles north of Los Angeles, it looks from the outside like a rustic sprawl. The day is a gold sliver in a navy sky.
INT. LAZY-T RANCH - BATHROOM - DAWN
Writer DALTON TRUMBO, 41, debonair, heartfelt and combative, is naked in a tub, his copyholder on a wood plank as the steam rises.
INT. LAZY-T RANCH - TRUMBO'S STUDY - DAWN

Like the whole home, beautifully appointed. Trumbo, kindled cigarette in its holder, attacks the keys of a typewriter on his desk, the fastest two-fingered typist ever as we -QUICKLY CUT AROUND HIS OFFICE, Taking in: - The American Booksellers National Book Award for his novel, Johnny Got His Gun. - The poster for his movie, Thirty Seconds Over Tokyo. - His Oscar nomination for the screenplay of Kitty Foyle. TIGHT ON TRUMBO'S TYPEWRITER As the inky letters CHOP movie dialogue across the white paper, Trumbo writing like a boxer working a speed bag -MANNY: What do you want? Thick with framed photos of: - Trumbo with KATHARINE HEPBURN at a United Refugee Committee dinner. - Trumbo as a World War II correspondent in his dress uniform, boarding a plane. - Trumbo in muddy fatigues, among battered SOLDIERS on a battle-scarred beach in the South Pacific. BACK TO A TIGHT CLOSE-UP OF TRUMBO'S WRITING Words racing across the page: What we all want. ATOP HIS DESK - FRAMED FAMILY PICTURES In them, WE SEE both Trumbo's furious pecking reflected on glass, animatedly overlaying stills under glass of: - Trumbo's wife Cleo in a stunning portrait. - Trumbo and Cleo with their three children, blowing out Niki's eighth birthday cake. BACK TO TRUMBO AT THE TYPEWRITER Lemony morning light now paints the windows. Trumbo writes:
And now we HEAR -EDWARD G. ROBINSON (PRE-LAP)
-- as the final words of Trumbo's speech strike paper -...or alone. -- Trumbo SLAPS the return and in a WHITE BLUR we're now -INT. A NEW YORK ALLEY (MGM SOUNDSTAGE) - NIGHT
...or alone.
TRUMBO - JOHN MCNAMARA

EDWARD G. ROBINSON -- a charismatic, stocky man of 53, both assertive and refined -- plays "MANNY," stepping from a sedan, moving toward a SUPPORTING PLAYER as "ROCCO," on his knees, bloody lip, torn jacket.
Manny, these guys... I don't give 'em what they're after, they'll kill me.
Hold it, Rocco.
"Manny" takes out his revolver -- and aims it at "Rocco," who freezes as he stands.
If we don't fight these guys, sure, maybe you -As "Manny" gestures with the gun, its cylinder dislodges from
the barrel -- and several bullets FLY out and comically CLATTER to the stage floor at Robinson's feet.
EDWARD G. ROBINSON/"MANNY" (CONT'D)
The director is out of his chair and on his feet -- SAM WOOD, early 60s, sharp, authoritative.
A BELL sounds.
Goin' again, Eddie. The CREW rustles in the shadows.
Sorry, Sam. Sorry, everybody. Of course, the one day the author's among us.
Trumbo sits nearby, in a bespoke suit, calmly smoking. Robinson settles in a canvas chair with his name on the back.
(as he glides past to talk to the CAMERAMAN) "Among us." Sure ain't one of us.
(keeping it light) What's the brilliant line, Trumbo?
"If we don't fight these guys, sure, maybe you get that long, happy life we all want."
Then what is it you've got me fighting for again?
"Peace on Earth, good will toward men."
Nearby, Wood SNORTS.
You can't do that, this is America.
How about sex and money?
There you go, two things we all love. None of your little sermons on citizenship.
EXT. A BEVERLY HILLS MANSION - BACKYARD - NIGHT

A flat half-acre dominated by a massive pool that GLOWS with a hundred floating candles. A PARTY where: - MEN are in black tie, WOMEN in gowns, everyone smokes, everyone drinks. Different time, different world. - There's MUSIC from a live BIG BAND. VOICES overlap and compete as we snag snippets:
"...I don't love it but Zanuck does..."
"...make the Indians the good guys, that's the twist..."

"Now the actors want to go on strike. Who's next? Lassie?" CLEO TRUMBO, 30s, hovers at the edge of a GROUP OF WOMEN about her age. TRUMBO - JOHN MCNAMARA She's beautiful, observant, sensitive, often silent, as she is here, excusing herself with a warm smile to look for someone at the party, passing by -A GROUP OF MEN. Louder, more boisterous. Edward G. Robinson. Within that group,
(in mid-sentence) ...still doing reshoots, what else? Luckily, Mayer lined up the A Team, Sam Wood to shoot, Dalton Trumbo for rewrites, so, fingers crossed...
From across the lawn, TWO MALE VOICES suddenly RISE, so we
...Jesus, Trumbo, a goddamn sixmonth strike, for what?
(dryly) Well, I think... money.

Cleo Trumbo turns to that second VOICE with minor dread and as she does, she and Robinson clock one another with the same thought: Jesus, here we go... As they both zero in on: Director Sam Wood, more than a little drunk.
Laugh it up. I had no crew! I couldn't work -(shouting at Trumbo) -- you wouldn't work, God forbid you cross a picket line. For set builders. What do set builders have to do with writing?

What writers write, builders build. What they build, you film. You make all the money you possibly can, so do I, why shouldn't they? And why can't we help them? In the long run, it's better for everyone -
-- said the Swimming Pool Soviet.

(calmly) Sam. You won. The strike's over, the union's history. We've all gone back to being good little worker bees making sweet movie honey and you -(now, just a little sharp) -- might just try being a gracious winner.
That last comes with a gentle poke into Wood's lapel from Trumbo's fingers, which hold his cigarette. Wood does not appreciate the jab or the accompanying smoke in his face.
It's never over with you people -strike, after strike, after strike!
Wood is SHOUTING now. Among the Guests: HEADS turn... SMILES falter... CONVERSATIONS stop.
Y'know what? I'm going on strike -against people WHO GO ON STRIKE!
And I won't cross your picket line, either.
Wood might just shove Trumbo now, he's so angry, but --- suddenly, Robinson is there to get Wood's arm in a friendly grasp.
(with jovial aplomb) Sam, Sam, Sam, we've got to talk about those scenes tomorrow, disaster, who the hell wrote that crap? (a wink at Trumbo, steering Wood away) But first, may I have this dance...?
At the same time, Cleo takes her husband's hand and moves him in the opposite direction.
TRUMBO - JOHN MCNAMARA
Having fun? Eternally.

They kiss and move to the bar, passing --- a tall, broad MAN of 39 who eyes Trumbo the way a western sheriff would a gunfighter. We don't know it yet, but: This is JOHN WAYNE. And as his eyes follow the Trumbos, he meets the gaze of a WOMAN. Late middle years, trim, striking, grand yet folksy, always in a stylish hat. Wayne and she share a moment of silent understanding. We don't know it yet, but: This is HEDDA HOPPER.
INT. LOS ANGELES MOVIE THEATRE 1 - NIGHT
UP ON THE MOVIE SCREEN - A NEWSREEL Actual black & white documentary footage of a waving WINSTON CHURCHILL, grimly addressing an assembly:
Nobody knows what Soviet Russia intends to do. An Iron Curtain has descended across the continent.
The newsreel CUTS TO a cartoon map of Europe and Asia as an animated red Iron Curtain drops, enclosing Soviet Russia.
The front line in a new kind of war. A Cold War! SHOTS of Russian military might: ARMIES march, FIGHTERS launch, TANKS roll.
The enemy -- Communists!
BACK TO the cartoon map as the Iron Curtain BECOMES a series of red tentacles -NEWSREEL NARRATOR (CONT'D)
TRUMBO - JOHN MCNAMARA

-- and those tentacles slither across Europe and Asia, heading for the United States. REVERSE TO REVEAL - THE MOVIE AUDIENCE MEN in ties, WOMEN in dresses, KIDS in their Sunday best. And among them WE FIND Dalton Trumbo with his family: daughter NIKI, 8, son CHRIS, 6, Cleo, and in her lap, their youngest, MITZI, 2, all munching and sipping movie treats, watching -THE NEWSREEL As it CUTS TO A SHOT OF the Hollywood sign -NEWSREEL NARRATOR (CONT'D) Dateline: Hollywood. The offices of former film starlet, now top columnist, Hedda Hopper. -- then DISSOLVES TO A SHOT of a newspaper column under the aerodynamically lettered byline, HEDDA HOPPER'S HOLLYWOOD. Next to the text, Hedda Hopper. inside her column: As she begins to speak from
(to camera) Greetings from our film capital, where all is sun and fun. Or is it?
CUT TO A WIDE SHOT (ARCHIVAL) -- OF WELL-KNOWN MOVIE FOLK. Grouped together a bit awkwardly in bright midday sun:
See the famous faces here? Danny Kaye, Humphrey Bogart, his dishy bride, Lauren Bacall, their good friend, film director John Huston, and many others...
CLOSE-UPS (ARCHIVAL) of these FAMOUS FACES, plus a few LESS
...all of whom have declared solidarity with film crews picketing for higher wages. In walk-outs that quickly turned violent.
STUDIOS that indeed turn violent: POLICE CLUB THE STRIKERS,
ATTACK DOGS MAUL, FIRE HOSES SPRAY -HEDDA HOPPER (V.O.) (CONT'D)

Because these strikes were in fact the work of dangerous radicals. A NEW CLOSE-UP (TO BE SHOT) -- SOMEONE standing on an apple box, addressing a peaceful line of studio PICKETERS: It's Dalton Trumbo, his black & white face 40 feet high:
...this is what angers and
frightens the studios!
DOWN IN THE THEATRE

The Trumbo family is silently stunned. Trumbo's own jaw slackens just a bit at the surreal sight of himself up on screen, then he quickly regains his composure. But his oldest child can't help herself -NIKI

He nods curtly, pats her arm and the tiny, color, real Trumbo down here watches: THE ENORMOUS BLACK & WHITE FACE OF TRUMBO - UP ON SCREEN Delivering this searing union rallying cry to the assembled, among whom we glimpse an APPLAUDING Edward G. Robinson:

We've discovered where our true power lies! Builders, cameramen, painters, drivers, writers, actors, directors! We are a single, indivisible brotherhood of workers. Bargain with a few of us, you bargain with all of us! Threaten one, you threaten us all and it's war! We are many but from this day forward -- we are one!
The Picketers and Movie Stars UP ON SCREEN begin to CHEER Trumbo as -HEDDA HOPPER (V.O.)
A new CLOSE-UP of Hedda Hopper in her office: HEDDA HOPPER (ON SCREEN) ...like many of those strikers -and their supporters -- a
BACK TO TRUMBO AND NIKI As daughter looks at father, surprised -- is that true? Trumbo doesn't meet her questioning gaze, just smokes and stoically takes in: HEDDA HOPPER - UP ON SCREEN Dwarfing him:
Which is why we must know: Who exactly was behind that walk-out... and why?
The scene now CUTS TO various CONGRESSMEN, INVESTIGATORS and AIDES mounting the stairs of the U.S. Capitol.
Our elected leaders will find out.
The CAMERA SINGLES OUT a bald, fifty-ish New Jerseyite -HEDDA HOPPER (V.O.) (CONT'D) Congressman J. Parnell Thomas and

Communism is not some faraway threat; its most dangerous agents are here, controlling the airwaves and movie screens, taking over its employees and their unions. They need to be identified as the enemies they are.
INT. MOVIE THEATRE - LOBBY - LATER THAT NIGHT
The five Trumbos stream out with the audience. suddenly -- A YOUNG FATHER WITH HIS KIDS
TRUMBO - JOHN MCNAMARA
It was, yes -The Father FLINGS Coke into Trumbo's face, SPLATTERING him -Traitor --
YOUNG FATHER -- terrifying Cleo and the kids before taking off.
(dripping, calm) No harm done, everybody okay?
EXT. LAZY-T RANCH - CORRAL - DAY
Mountain, meadow and sky dwarf this fine spread. Trumbo walks along the fence while Niki rides a horse next to him.
So, are you a Communist? I am.
Is it against the law? It is not.
That lady with the big hat said you're a "dangerous radical." Are you?
Radical, maybe. Dangerous, only to men who fling Cokes.
You don't want to overthrow the government?
No, we have a good government. But anything good can be better, don't you think?
Is Mom a Communist?
No. Am I?
Let's give you the official test. Mom packs your favorite lunch... Salami.
...and you see someone at school with no lunch -- what do you do? Share?
You don't tell them to get a job? No.
(aware she's being teased)
Offer a loan at six percent? Dad.
Then just ignore them. No.
He studies his oldest daughter with great affection.
Well, well. You little Commie.
INT. EDWARD G. ROBINSON'S MANSION - LIVING ROOM - NIGHT

Elegant, tasteful, its walls glowing colorfully with: Six spotlit French Impressionist paintings. Seated under a Renoir, Trumbo is debating with Robinson and a small gathering of DIRECTORS, PRODUCERS, ACTORS and WRITERS.
TRUMBO - JOHN MCNAMARA
Trumbo, as usual, you only make sense to you. Let 'em call you a Communist, me a Democrat -- both legitimate parties, yours is just meaner and duller but nothing illegal about any of it. Yet.
Hunter, tell your friend to dial down the paranoia and have a drink.
This to screenwriter IAN McLELLAN HUNTER, 32, quick mind, sad eyes, sharp wit, always puffing on a pipe:
I don't think he's being paranoid enough.
Not you, too.
Trumbo pulls several booklets of mimeoed paper from a valise.
I've drawn up a little pamphlet. (passing them out) Subject: "Congress Unaware of Little Thing Called First Amendment."
They're aware...
Everyone turns to the man saying:
...they just don't give a shit.
ARLEN HIRD is 40s, caustic, dyspeptic; the BUTLER offering him a canape, which he waves off -ARLEN HIRD (CONT'D)
And anybody who thinks it's about movies is an idiot. Which is why I'm going to go have a chat with the other side.
You don't mean the Alliance. I do.
A CHORUS of disbelieving MOANS and startled LAUGHS.
Are you out of your mind -- ?
They're actors, writers, directors, just like us -EDWARD G. ROBINSON They're Nazis, they're just too
cheap to buy the uniforms -IAN MCLELLAN HUNTER They invited Congress out here -DALTON TRUMBO (to the room at large) And are they all Sam Wood and Hedda
Go for it. Be fun. 'Cause ya know who you're gonna be talkin' to?
I wanna say one thing about a place I love. No, not Hollywood...
And WE REVEAL WE'RE -INT. HOLLYWOOD ROOSEVELT HOTEL - BANQUET ROOM - NIGHT
...I like Hollywood...
Alone on stage, John Wayne, six-feet, four inches of bassvoiced movie icon.
...but I love America! As WE REVEAL a sign near the stage announcing this is:
OF AMERICAN IDEALS WE SEE the HUGE CROWD of movie pros, from below-the-line crew to above-the-line stars, all CHEERING and APPLAUDING Wayne.

And when we talk about America I'm talking about freedom! The kinda freedom we just fought a world war to save! You wanna be a Commie, go be a Commie... but some friends of mine in Washington think you got some questions to answer!
AT THE BACK OF THE ROOM
Trumbo, Hird, Hunter and Edward G. Robinson all stand, studying Wayne, awed and disturbed by his power.
I never knew he was this good.
'Cause he's not acting, that's him.
UP ON STAGE Wayne's voice RISES with the MOB'S CHEERS.
Still wanna be a Commie? Be a Commie! In Russia! But off ya go 'n' enjoy the Bolshoi Ballet!
INT. HOLLYWOOD ROOSEVELT HOTEL - LOBBY - LATER THAT NIGHT

Trumbo, Hird, Robinson and Hunter offer copies of Trumbo's pamphlet to THOSE who ignore or shoot them dirty looks. Or put on their hats and throw smug smiles. Including -SAM WOOD (as he accepts a pamphlet)
Russian for "goodbye," as he tears it to shreds.

segment-speaker: TRUMBO - JOHN MCNAMARA EDWARD G. ROBINSON
segment-text: Is a nightmare.
If it were, I'd be in bed.
An elegantly turned out, in-the-flesh Hedda Hopper approaches.
Hello, Dalton.
Hedda, here you are, of course. Good evening to you.
Eddie, darling. Hedda.
New hat?
Daily, dear, daily. (to Trumbo) Been to the movies lately? (before he can answer) Duke!
John Wayne has crossed the room with IATSE Union Leader ROY BREWER, 40, jowly, jovial and shrewd.
Mag. Nificent.
Just sayin' what needs sayin'. (then) Hiya, Eddie. Duke.
Hear you 'n' your pals got a "pamphlet." Any takers? Not yet...

segment-speaker: EDWARD G. ROBINSON TRUMBO - JOHN MCNAMARA ARLEN HIRD
segment-text: (offers one to Wayne) Would you like one? We're Communists.
(shrugs) He's a writer.
(to Robinson) Ya won't get any takers. Why not? Not here.
Wayne now turns and regards an affable Trumbo.
All it says is, Congress has no right to investigate how we vote, where we pray, what we think, say or make movies about. (holds out the pamphlet) Hello, I'm Dalton Trumbo.
(takes the pamphlet) Congress has the right to go after anything they think is a threat.
We disagree. That's the point -we both have the right to be wrong.
A LOOSE, CURIOUS GROUP has begun to gather, sensing trouble.
You wanna talk about rights, first show me whose side you're on. Russia's no friend, not anymore. You better wake up. (then) 'Cause it's a new day. A new day.
Maybe it's not for your kind.
My kind, what kind is that?
Kind that has no idea why we just won a war.
Wayne crumples Trumbo's pamphlet, tosses it and starts off.

That's the second time you've brought that up. I was a war correspondent in Okinawa -(to Hedda now) -- your son was stationed in the Philippines -(re: Robinson) -- Eddie was in Europe with the Office of War Information -(back to Wayne) -- where'd you serve again?
(stops, turns) You tryin' to say something?

No, Duke, he wasn't -HEDDA HOPPER Stay out of it, Eddie -DALTON TRUMBO (right in Wayne's face) If you're gonna talk about World War Two like you personally won it, let's be clear where you were stationed -- on a film set, shooting blanks, wearing makeup and if you're going to hit me, I'd like to take my glasses off.
Wayne could belt him but camera FLASHBULBS POP.
Duke, let's get outta here...
He urges the apoplectic Wayne to a doorway.
Thank you, Dalton. My next column just wrote itself.
She exits. Robinson, Hird and Hunter just stare at Trumbo.
(happy as a clam) That was... I don't even know what to...
Yes, good thing you don't want to demonize anybody or that could've been awkward.
EXT. MGM STUDIOS - DAY

Walking with Robinson, Trumbo crosses the gleaming, BUSY dream factory at the height of its productivity, stops at a newsstand to buy cigarettes and is confronted by a strange and awful sight: The face of Hedda Hopper on the July 28, 1947 cover of TIME magazine, the copies all hung in a line, creating two dozen identical Heddas. Trumbo is quietly amused, Robinson slightly horrified.
Good God. (reaches for a copy) Trumbo, you pick your enemies the way you live -- only the best will do.
INT. MGM STUDIOS - LOUIS B. MAYER'S OFFICE - DAY
LOUIS B. MAYER, 60, warm-hearted, cold-blooded studio president, beams affectionately at Trumbo.
Your next deal's gonna make you the highest-paid writer in Hollywood, which'll make you the highest-paid writer in the world.
BUDDY ROSS, early 30s, a young, go-getter producer, sits off to the side, eyes darting anxiously between the studio president and the writer.
You earned it. You don't just write happy endings, you actually believe them.
That's what the people pay to see, that's why you belong here at MGM, right, L.B.?
Look, I hate to make the wooing too easy for you but: Where do I sign?
Just one thing. If you're going to work for me... (unfolds a newspaper) ...I never, ever want to see anything like this...
HEDDA HOPPER'S HOLLYWOOD column: a photo of Dalton Trumbo under the headline, "OUR OWN RED MENACE." ...again.
LOUIS B. MAYER (CONT'D)
Trumbo and Mayer lock eyes.
Buddy is frozen silent.
I promise.
Mayer nods, satisfied. Buddy could kiss Trumbo.
Just stop reading Hedda Hopper.
Off Mayer's fury and Buddy's panic -EXT. LAZY-T RANCH - TERRACE - DAY

Overlooking their lake. Trumbo stands at a hot grill, flipping filet of trout, holding court for his family; Hunter, his WIFE and CHILDREN; Robinson and his WIFE; Buddy Ross and a GLAMOROUS INGENUE GIRLFRIEND.
(to Cleo) Mom, please...?
(to Niki) Not now, honey.
So what'd L.B. say then?
Jesus, don't, I haven't eaten since. My job's not hard enough?
Your job's not hard at all.
TRUMBO - JOHN MCNAMARA
You think getting Mayer to sign you after those headlines was easy? You have a record-breaking, threeyear contract -- to make shit up. You're welcome.
C'mon, Mom!
EXT. A NARROW DIRT ROAD - DAY
A solitary black sedan churns up a plume of dust.
EXT. LAZY-T RANCH - TERRACE - DAY
Trumbo and the other adults LAUGH and drink away a dazzling summer afternoon.
(to Robinson) She really can! Ask her!
(chuckling) Cleo? Is this true?
I... had a very unusual mother.
Which means "Stage Mother."
He picks up a glass, a twinkle in his eye.
(knows what's coming, laughs, embarrassed) Trumbo, no...

He hands Niki the glass, who tosses it to Cleo, which she catches easily, then another, which she catches in her other hand, beginning to lightly JUGGLE the two, causing the children to finally SQUEAL with delight as -EXT. ANOTHER DIRT ROAD - DAY -- that black sedan takes a gravel-popping turn and --
TRUMBO - JOHN MCNAMARA EXT. LAZY-T RANCH - TERRACE - DAY
-- Robinson and his Wife stare in amazement, watching Cleo expertly JUGGLE two glasses, flipping and catching one behind her back as Trumbo beams.
The misspent youth of a child acrobat.
My God, how long were you in show business?
(juggling away) Till I was 15. And it was more "show-forced-labor."
Niki tosses her mother a third glass, which Cleo catches, now pinwheeling all three easily.
She never drops one, ever.
Mom had a little saying. "Drop it, you lose your next meal."
(to Robinson) And "Mom" wasn't kidding. Woman was a Dickensian harridan.
(laughs) No one even knows what that means.
EXT. LAZY-T RANCH - DRIVEWAY - DAY The black sedan closes in -EXT. LAZY-T RANCH - TERRACE - DAY

-- as Cleo does over-hand grabs, then switches to a circular pattern, the spool of tumblers catching sunlight in dazzling prisms. AND THROUGH CLEO'S GLASSY WHIRL OF COLOR - WE SEE THAT SEDAN Pull to a stop and Trumbo sees THREE MEN in suits get out.
TRUMBO - JOHN MCNAMARA EXT. LAZY-T RANCH - DRIVEWAY - A FEW MOMENTS LATER
Family and friends watching, Trumbo greets the three men, among whom we recognize: HUAC Investigator Robert Stripling.
Mr. Dalton Trumbo? Stripling hands Trumbo a pink document.
INT. U.S. CAPITOL - ROTUNDA - DAY
Congressman J. Parnell Thomas stands before REPORTERS:
Nineteen subpoenas have been issued to those we believe have knowledge of the ongoing Communist threat in Hollywood.
This announcement is greeted with a resounding... pause.
Uh, what kind of threat is that?
A conspiracy to corrupt democratic values and bring about the overthrow of this nation.
Using... movies.
The CHUCKLING Reporters clearly think this is horseshit.
Any movie in particular or...?
(cutting him off) Movies are the most powerful form of influence ever created and they are infested with hidden traitors who will be dragged into the light, for all to see and all to judge.
HEDDA HOPPER'S HOLLYWOOD NEWSREEL
Her talking face again appears alongside her column.
(to camera) We travel now...
EXT./INT. U.S. CAPITOL - VARIOUS SHOTS - DAY - THE HEARINGS
And actual newsreel footage in black & white of the Capitol packed with POLITICIANS, EXECUTIVES and STARS.
...to Washington, D.C., where battle lines are drawn. On one
And in actual footage from his testimony that day, RONALD REAGAN, 36, handsome and measured, cooperates with Robert Stripling before a PACKED HOUSE.
Your profession, Mr. Reagan?
Motion picture actor.
And you are currently the president of the Screen Actors Guild? Yes, sir.
Has it been reported to you that certain members of the guild were Communists?
Yes, sir, I have heard different discussions of some of them as Communists.
More actual news footage -- CONGRESSMAN RICHARD M. NIXON (RCalifornia), 34.
TRUMBO - JOHN MCNAMARA
Do you believe that the motion picture industry is doing everything it can to rid itself of subversive, Un-American influences? Then answering, in testimony we recreate in matching black &
No! The Communists are everywhere! They report directly to Moscow!
Enough is enough! The Communists have to go!
What we're about to do...
INT. LAZY-T RANCH - LIVING ROOM - DAY - BEFORE THE HEARINGS
...won't make us too popular.
Trumbo leads a small strategy session with Robert Kenny, Arlen Hird and a HALF-DOZEN of the SUBPOENAED 19 and --- Niki, in a corner, quietly taking everything in.
Our support's not only going to vanish, we're going to get attacked by our own.
Okay, we get slandered, then what?
You testify and answer every question they ask, in your own way.
So don't tell 'em shit.
Beautifully put.
TRUMBO - JOHN MCNAMARA
In her corner, Niki smiles.
INT. U.S. CAPITOL - DAY - THE HEARINGS
Of the nineteen forced to appear, only ten were called to the stand.
The Hollywood Nineteen wait to be called to testify.
Dubbed "The Hollywood Ten," they refused to answer every question about their Communist ties.
You know what that's called?
INT. LAZY-T RANCH - LIVING ROOM - DAY - BEFORE THE HEARINGS
Contempt of Congress. Beat.
The room is silent, Niki transfixed. Then --
Maybe I'm missing something, 'cause all I'm seein' here is jail.
INT. U.S. CAPITOL - CAUCUS ROOM - DAY - THE HEARINGS
As Trumbo is sworn in, there's a BLINDING CAMERA FLASH and Congress, the crowds and Trumbo all BURST INTO FULL COLOR -STRIPLING Mr. Trumbo, I will ask various questions, all of which can be
I shall answer yes or no if I please to. Many questions can only be answered yes or no by a moron or a slave.
As the caucus room FLUTTERS with DISBELIEVING MOANS, supportive LAUGHTER and a few nasty BOOS, WE FIND --
TRUMBO - JOHN MCNAMARA
Cleo Trumbo, in the gallery, serenely unsurprised by her husband's insolence.
INT. LAZY-T RANCH - LIVING ROOM - DAY - BEFORE THE HEARINGS
No one can beat Congress in Congress. The only place to do that's in court -ARLEN HIRD -- in front of a judge who'll probably hate Commies and rule
-- in a lower court, yes, we'll likely lose but on appeal...
...it'll work. The Supreme Court is a five to four liberal majority. They think the Committee's unconstitutional, they want it killed and we're gonna hand them the case to do it. Great.
Best of luck.
And suddenly, Hird is on his feet and gone.
EXT. LAZY-T RANCH - TERRACE/LAKE - DAY - BEFORE THE HEARINGS

Mitzi SPLASHES in the water with Chris as Cleo aims a large, professional camera at them and snaps pictures. Arlen Hird walks to a nearby tree, leans against it and stabs a cigarette into his mouth. Trumbo walks up.
(sighs, beat) Look, I can't afford what you're talkin' about... Supreme Court, legal fees. My wife, it's never been great, but I raid our savings for this? She's gone, with both boys.
TRUMBO - JOHN MCNAMARA
I'll cover you. Expenses, travel, legal fees...
Hird is shocked by Trumbo's casual generosity.
You don't even like me.
I like you fine, you don't like me.
Also, I don't trust you.
I'd say "go on," but I'm afraid you will.
Look. I know what I am, okay? I want this country to be a whole new... everything. Top to bottom. And if I get what I want? Nobody
gets their own lake.
Seems a little grim.

For you, yeah. But not the guys who built all this... (the ranch, the lake) ...for you, their families, friends. Which, remember, is kind of the point. Them, not you. I mean, if I'm wrong here, tell me, but ever since I've known you, you talk like a radical, live like a rich guy -True.

Each 'n' every morning.
